paper_title: Dynamic SDN-Based Radio Access Network Slicing With Deep Reinforcement Learning for URLLC and eMBB Services
paper_claims: The global two-level resource block allocation for URLLC and eMBB with inter-gNodeB borrowing, OFDMA exclusivity, per-user resource limits, eMBB minimum rates, and URLLC delay bounds constitutes a non-linear binary optimization problem that is NP-hard via reduction from the 0–1 knapsack problem.

A two time-scale SDN-based RAN slicing mechanism that allocates resource blocks to gNodeBs using a single-agent EXP3 bandit and performs per-user scheduling and cooperative inter-gNodeB resource sharing using multi-agent deep Q-learning dynamically adapts to traffic and channel variations while limiting controller–gNodeB signaling.
Packet delays for URLLC and eMBB traffic are modeled with an M/M/1 queuing system, and minimum-rate and maximum-delay constraints are enforced within the allocation and learning processes.
Support for multiple 5G NR OFDM numerologies is achieved by selecting numerology-specific trained models at the gNodeB based on channel state information.

paper_title: V-BOINC: The Virtualization of BOINC
paper_claims: Running BOINC applications inside lightweight virtual machines that embed a BOINC client enables execution on heterogeneous volunteer hosts without per-architecture porting, while providing sandbox isolation and system-level checkpointing and recovery.

Mountable dependency disks carrying application software stacks allow BOINC applications with complex dependencies to run without modifying host environments and without inflating the base virtual machine image.

Using headless VirtualBox with bridged networking and guest control APIs satisfies the need for unique guest addressing, remote management, and VM snapshotting while maintaining user transparency on Linux and macOS.
Performance differences relative to native BOINC are primarily due to virtualization-imposed resource constraints, with negligible additional overhead introduced by the V-BOINC control layer.

Periodic virtual machine snapshots preserve job progress across host interruptions with typically low storage and time overheads for CPU-bound workloads, increasing for memory- or disk-intensive tasks.

paper_title: Distributionally Robust Bayesian Quadrature Optimization
paper_claims: Maximizing the expected integrand under the worst-case distribution within a χ2-divergence ball around the empirical context distribution defines a distributionally robust Bayesian quadrature objective that reduces to a variance-regularized risk in a high-variance regime, admits efficient inner minimization via KKT-based bisection, and can be optimized by posterior sampling with sublinear Bayesian regret under standard kernel assumptions.

paper_title: Self-Aware Personalized Federated Learning
paper_claims: Personalized federated learning is captured by a two-level Bayesian hierarchical formulation in which inter-client and intra-client uncertainties determine posterior-optimal personal and global targets and thus the trade-off between local adaptation and global sharing.

An uncertainty-aware federated procedure that initializes client models toward a variance-weighted global estimate, uses adaptive early stopping based on estimated uncertainties, and aggregates updates with variance weights converges to the client-specific FL-optimal and global-optimal parameters under a two-level Gaussian model.

Inter- and intra-client uncertainties can be approximated online in deep models by empirical variances of local and global parameter trajectories across rounds, enabling automatic per-client determination of local training steps and discrepancy-aware aggregation without additional communication or computation beyond standard FedAvg.

The uncertainty-based formulation recovers standard federated averaging when inter-client variation is negligible and reduces to local-only learning when clients are weakly connected or a single client’s data quality dominates, thereby self-tuning the degree of personalization.

Uncertainty-driven initialization, adaptive local training durations, and variance-weighted aggregation collectively improve personalized performance across heterogeneous clients relative to sample-size-based aggregation and fixed-step schedules.

paper_title: Adversarial Machine Learning for 5G Communications Security
paper_claims: In CBRS-style 5G spectrum sharing, an adversary can train a surrogate classifier from local sensing and ACK observations to predict successful transmissions and selectively jam either the sensing phase to force misdetections or the data phase to cause failures, with sensing-phase jamming being stealthier and more energy-efficient despite no direct access to the target model’s inputs or outputs.

A generative adversarial network configured with distributed generator and discriminator nodes can synthesize RF waveforms that mimic a legitimate 5G user’s signals and thereby evade deep-learning-based physical-layer authentication used for network slicing.

Intentionally flipping a small, carefully selected subset of high-confidence machine-learning decisions in 5G control (e.g., sensing or access outcomes) increases an attacker’s surrogate-model uncertainty and mitigates subsequent evasion and poisoning attacks while limiting performance degradation.

paper_claims: Coordinated checkpoint I/O in HPC exhibits irregular spatial and temporal patterns partitioned into application data, library, and shared-memory zones whose sizes respectively shrink with increased parallelism, remain approximately constant per application, and grow with processes per node, making storage time a function of zone size and access patterns and enabling regression-based estimation of checkpoint duration and overhead-constrained checkpoint frequency when node congestion is negligible.

paper_title: An Empirical Study of Recent Face Alignment Methods
paper_claims: The area under the cumulative error distribution curve up to a specified threshold (AUC-α) serves as a robust, outlier-insensitive single-value metric for evaluating face alignment performance over datasets compared to mean error.

Face alignment accuracy is highly sensitive to the initial face bounding box, with small center perturbations generally causing larger degradation than comparable scale changes and with substantial variability in robustness across methods.

Mismatch between the face detector used for training and the detector used at test time significantly degrades alignment performance even when bounding boxes have high overlap, making detector consistency essential for fair evaluation and practical deployment.

In cascaded holistic face alignment, including a mean-shape initialization at test time and aggregating multiple initializations improves localization accuracy, and increasing cascade depth yields non-decreasing performance gains at the expense of computational cost.

Evaluating with tight ground-truth face boxes can overestimate robustness, whereas benchmarking with practical detector outputs (including recovered missing detections) reveals method sensitivities and meaningful performance differences.

paper_title: Efficient Non-greedy Optimization of Decision Trees
paper_claims: Casting oblique decision tree induction as structured prediction with latent binary variables permits a joint optimization of all split and leaf parameters via a convex–concave surrogate upper bound on empirical loss for general loss functions.

Constraining loss-augmented inference to a Hamming ball of radius one around the current split decisions yields O(d^2 p) per-sample gradient computation while preserving standard O(dp) inference at test time.
Row-wise norm constraints on split weights control the tightness of the surrogate bound, prevent degenerate scaling, and provide regularization that implicitly prunes unused leaves.
Non-greedy optimization under this framework produces decision trees that generalize better than greedy baselines on diverse multiclass classification benchmarks.

paper_title: Fuzzy Jaccard Index: A robust comparison of ordered lists
paper_claims: The Fuzzy Jaccard Index is a scale-invariant similarity measure for ranked lists that replaces binary membership with fuzzy membership based on relative ranking scores and is bounded in [0,1] with value 1 if and only if the two rankings are identical.

Under the standard Jaccard index the least similar ranking to a given ranking is its exact reverse, whereas for the Fuzzy Jaccard index no global minimizer exists for k<n unless the score multiset is fixed, in which case the reverse ordering minimizes similarity.

The area under the Fuzzy Jaccard curve over k provides a compact [0,1]-bounded summary of similarity that satisfies AUC_Fuji being at least AUC_Jaccard for any pair of orderings and converges to the Jaccard AUC in the infinite-gap limit.
